original_hypothesis.title: Tokenomics.checkpoint() Lacks Reentrancy Guard Despite External Call to Treasury Before Epoch Counter Increment
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Tokenomics.checkpoint() makes an external call to ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) before incrementing epochCounter. The contract has a _locked reentrancy variable initialized in the constructor, but checkpoint() neither checks nor modifies it. If the treasury contract is compromised or malicious, it could re-enter checkpoint() during rebalanceTreasury. Since epochCounter hasn't been incremented yet, the reentrancy would process the same epoch data again, potentially corrupting epoch settlement (doubled rewards, inflated effectiveBond, etc.).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The _locked variable exists in Tokenomics but is never used in checkpoint(). The external call to treasury occurs late in the function but before the critical epochCounter increment. While treasury is a trusted admin-set contract, the absence of reentrancy protection is a code defect that could have severe consequences if the trust assumption is violated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _locked is set to 1 in initializeTokenomics but never checked in checkpoint(); ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) is called before epochCounter = uint32(eCounter + 1)

preconditions: Treasury contract is compromised or intentionally malicious
The re-entrant call passes the delegatecall check, same-block check, and epoch time check

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _locked exists but is not used in checkpoint(); external call before state finalization
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During the rebalanceTreasury external call window

operational_questions: Would a reentrancy in checkpoint actually succeed given the delegatecall and timing checks?
What state corruption would result from double-processing an epoch?
Is the treasury contract immutable or upgradeable?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock treasury that re-enters checkpoint during rebalanceTreasury
Verify whether the second checkpoint call passes all guards
Measure state corruption if reentrancy succeeds

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise treasury contract or set a malicious treasury address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Malicious treasury re-enters checkpoint() during rebalanceTreasury callback
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Nested checkpoint processes same epoch again with partially-updated state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: epochCounter incremented twice or state variables double-counted
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires control over treasury contract (admin compromise)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inflation accounting, effectiveBond, and reward distribution corrupted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Very difficult - requires understanding exact state corruption

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - requires compromising a trusted contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusual treasury behavior during rebalanceTreasury calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: epochCounter incrementing by more than 1 in a single block
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple checkpoint-related events in a single transaction

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
